summary: Genesis 1:26 exhibits multiple theologically motivated revisions centered on the divine plural 'Let us make' and the phrase 'in our image, after our likeness.' Targumic traditions engage in extensive anthropomorphism avoidance by replacing 'image' language with references to divine glory or created order, while also reflecting proto-rabbinic concern to foreclose christological readings of the plural. The LXX introduces subtle harmonization with Hellenistic philosophical vocabulary, and the Peshitta shows awareness of anti-anthropomorphic sensitivities.
summary_plain: Translators and scribes were deeply concerned about two things in this verse: the plural 'Let us make' (which could suggest multiple gods or persons in God) and the idea that humans are made 'in God's image' (which could imply God has a physical body). Different traditions solved these problems in different ways—some changed 'image' to mean something less physical, others added interpretive glosses to explain away the plural.

revisions.mt_reading: Let us make humanity in our image (בְּצַלְמֵנוּ), according to our likeness (כִּדְמוּתֵנוּ)
revisions.revised_reading: Targum Neofiti: 'I shall create man in my image'; Targum Pseudo-Jonathan adds extensive interpretive expansion specifying that the image refers to understanding and intellect rather than physical form, and includes reference to angels as interlocutors to explain the plural
revisions.evidence: The Targumic tradition systematically reinterprets צֶלֶם/דְּמוּת to avoid the implication that God possesses corporeality. Neofiti's shift from plural to singular ('I shall create') eliminates the problematic plural entirely. Pseudo-Jonathan's expansion—introducing angels as conversation partners and specifying intellectual rather than physical resemblance—represents classic anti-anthropomorphic exegesis. This cannot be mere translation variance because the MT terms are straightforward and require no clarification; the additions are clearly interpretive glosses addressing theological anxiety.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translations change the troubling phrase 'in our image' by either removing the plural 'our' entirely or adding explanations that God is talking to angels and that 'image' means intellectual capacity, not physical appearance. These are clearly theological fixes, not just translation choices.
scholarly_citations: Hayward, Targumic Traditions and Genesis 1:26-27, JJS 1988
van der Heide, Anthropomorphisms in the Targums, in The Aramaic Bible, p. 45
revisions.counter_arguments: One could argue that Targumic expansions represent standard pedagogical practice rather than defensive theology, and that specifying 'intellectual image' simply makes explicit what was already implied in the Hebrew semantic range of צֶלֶם.

revisions.revised_reading: Targum Pseudo-Jonathan introduces the Memra (Word) formula and references consultation with the Torah/Wisdom as pre-existent entity, with added note that God consulted the ministering angels; Fragment Targum adds that God took counsel from the works of heaven and earth
revisions.evidence: The introduction of Memra theology and the identification of divine interlocutors represent proto-rabbinic strategies to defend monotheism against christological interpretation while explaining the plural. These additions reflect rabbinic midrashic traditions (GenR 8:3-8) that debate whom God addressed. The Fragment Targum's reference to 'works of heaven and earth' echoes the rabbinic interpretation that God consulted the creation itself. This is exegetical theology, not translation—the MT's simple נַעֲשֶׂה requires no grammatical expansion.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic versions add entire theological explanations not present in Hebrew: they say God was talking to his 'Word' (Memra) or to angels or even to the creation itself. These additions come from early rabbinic debates about how to explain the plural while maintaining that there is only one God, and they preemptively block Christian claims that the plural proves the Trinity.
scholarly_citations: Mulder, Theological and Ideological Tendencies in Targum Neofiti, JSOT 1985
Flesher & Chilton, The Targums: A Critical Introduction, p. 234
revisions.counter_arguments: The Memra formula may be a standard Targumic technique for preserving divine transcendence without specific polemical intent, and rabbinic identification of angelic interlocutors could reflect genuine exegetical puzzlement about the plural rather than anti-Christian polemic (which would be anachronistic for earliest Targumic layers).

revisions.mt_reading: in our image (בְּצַלְמֵנוּ), according to our likeness (כִּדְמוּתֵנוּ)
revisions.revised_reading: κατ' εἰκόνα ἡμετέραν καὶ καθ' ὁμοίωσιν—employing philosophical Greek terminology (εἰκών/ὁμοίωσις) that harmonizes with Hellenistic conceptual frameworks
revisions.evidence: While the LXX preserves the MT's dual-term structure, the choice of εἰκών and ὁμοίωσις imports Platonic philosophical vocabulary that reframes the Hebrew terms within a Greek conceptual matrix. Philo's subsequent interpretation (Opif. 69-71) demonstrates how these terms enabled allegorical reading that distinguished the archetypal εἰκών from material likeness. The translator's lexical choices facilitate harmonization with Hellenistic anthropology while avoiding the crudely physical implications צֶלֶם might carry. However, these could also be straightforward lexical equivalents without theological loading.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek translation uses philosophical Greek words ('eikon' and 'homoiosis') that were familiar to educated Greek readers from Plato and other philosophers. This allowed the verse to be read through Greek philosophical lenses, where 'image' could mean an ideal mental form rather than physical resemblance. But these might just be the best available Greek words.
scholarly_citations: Schaper, Eschatology in the Greek Psalter, p. 15 (on LXX theological translation technique)
Runia, Philo and the Timaeus, p. 134
Martin, The Antecedents of the Christian Doctrine of the Image of God, JTS 1982
revisions.counter_arguments: εἰκών and ὁμοίωσις are the most natural Greek equivalents for צֶלֶם and דְּמוּת respectively, and their use need not imply intentional philosophical harmonization. The philosophical overtones may be accidents of the Greek semantic field rather than deliberate translator choice.

revisions.mt_reading: in our image (בְּצַלְמֵנוּ), according to our likeness (כִּדְמוּתֵנוּ)
revisions.revised_reading: b-ṣalman w-ak-dmutan—largely literal but contextual usage in Syriac theological tradition shows awareness of anthropomorphic concerns; some manuscripts show minor variations suggesting interpretive transmission
revisions.evidence: The Peshitta rendering is relatively literal, but manuscript variation and subsequent Syriac patristic exegesis (Ephrem, Commentary on Genesis I.28-29) reveal awareness of the anthropomorphic problem. Ephrem explicitly interprets ṣalmā as referring to rational soul and sovereignty rather than physical form. While the Peshitta text itself shows less overt revision than Targum, its embedding within a Syriac interpretive tradition that emphasizes spiritual rather than physical 'image' suggests translational choices were made with anti-anthropomorphic sensibilities. However, the evidence of revision in the base text itself is limited.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Syriac translation is fairly literal, but early Syriac Christian interpreters like Ephrem make clear they understood 'image' to mean the spiritual, rational part of humanity, not physical appearance. The translators likely had this understanding in mind, though they didn't change the text as drastically as the Aramaic versions did.
scholarly_citations: Mathews, Genesis 1-11:26 (on Peshitta translation approach), p. 89
Brock, The Syriac Fathers on Prayer and the Spiritual Life, p. 34
Kronholm, Motifs from Genesis 1-11 in the Genuine Hymns of Ephrem, p. 56
revisions.counter_arguments: The Peshitta shows no textual revision at all in this verse—it is a straightforward rendering. Any theological concerns are read into it from later patristic commentary, not evident in the translation itself. This should probably not be classified as a theologically motivated textual change.

revisions.mt_reading: in our image (בְּצַלְמֵנוּ), according to our likeness (כִּדְמוּתֵנוּ)
revisions.revised_reading: ad imaginem et similitudinem nostram—Jerome's Latin preserves the dual terminology but harmonizes with established Latin theological vocabulary that had already received Trinitarian interpretation
revisions.evidence: Jerome's choice of 'ad imaginem et similitudinem' follows Old Latin tradition but ratifies vocabulary that Latin fathers (Tertullian, Augustine) had already charged with Trinitarian meaning, particularly regarding the plural 'nostram.' While Jerome claims to translate from Hebrew (Hebraica veritas), his lexical choices align with Latin theological tradition that read the plural as intimating the Trinity. Augustine's extensive exegesis (De Trinitate XII-XIV) exploiting the imago/similitudo distinction demonstrates how Jerome's rendering facilitated Trinitarian harmonization. Yet Jerome may simply have retained familiar Latin equivalents without intending theological loading.
revisions.evidence_plain: Jerome's Latin translation uses words ('imago' and 'similitudo') that Latin-speaking Christians had already been using for centuries to discuss the Trinity and the nature of humanity. His choice reinforced the idea that the plural 'our' hints at the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. But he may have just been using standard Latin vocabulary without any special theological agenda.
scholarly_citations: Kamesar, Jerome, Greek Scholarship, and the Hebrew Bible, p. 123
Brown, Augustine of Hippo, p. 287 (on De Trinitate and imago Dei)
revisions.counter_arguments: Jerome explicitly claimed fidelity to the Hebrew original and rejected allegorical excess. His lexical choices likely reflect standard Latin equivalency rather than deliberate Trinitarian harmonization, and the Trinitarian reading was imposed by later readers (Augustine) rather than intended by Jerome.

dominant_strategy: Anthropomorphism avoidance is the dominant revision strategy in Genesis 1:26 across traditions. The Targumic witnesses show the most extensive and explicit anti-anthropomorphic reinterpretation, systematically eliminating or explaining away the physical implications of 'image' and 'likeness.' This reflects a broader Second Temple and rabbinic anxiety about divine corporeality that intensified in response to both internal Jewish theological development and external polemical pressures. The Peshitta and LXX show more subtle strategies that enable non-corporeal interpretation without extensive textual expansion, while the Vulgate's choices facilitate later theological elaboration within Christian tradition.
overall_assessment: The textual history of Genesis 1:26 reveals a multi-directional theological contestation centered on two cruxes: the divine plural and the imago Dei. Targumic traditions deploy the most aggressive revisionist strategies, reflecting proto-rabbinic concerns to defend monotheism, avoid anthropomorphism, and potentially counter christological exegesis. The LXX's lexical choices facilitate Hellenistic philosophical harmonization, enabling allegorical interpretation that became central to Alexandrian exegesis. The Peshitta and Vulgate show more conservative textual approaches but embed their renderings within interpretive traditions that read specific theologies into relatively literal translations. The overall pattern demonstrates that Genesis 1:26 became a hermeneutical battlefield where fundamental questions of divine nature, human essence, and monotheistic integrity were negotiated through textual-interpretive strategies. The verse's very anthropological centrality made it a site of intensive theological management across all major traditions.
overall_plain: Genesis 1:26 became a theological hot spot because it says two things that worried ancient translators: God speaks in the plural ('Let us make') and humans are made in God's 'image.' The Aramaic translations added the most explanations to fix these problems—explaining that God was talking to angels and that 'image' means intellect, not body. The Greek and Latin translations were more subtle but used philosophical language that let readers interpret the verse in less physical ways. Each tradition was trying to protect its core beliefs: Jews defended monotheism and avoided suggesting God has a body, while Christians later used the same verse to support belief in the Trinity.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Targumic Anti-Anthropomorphism Blocks Christological Plural Interpretation
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The most significant finding is that Targumic revision strategies serve a dual theological function: they simultaneously avoid divine corporeality and preemptively foreclose Christian plural-as-Trinity readings. This suggests that by the time of the Targumim's final redaction, the synagogue was acutely aware of christological exegesis and deployed anti-anthropomorphic hermeneutics as a defensive strategy. The proto-rabbinic identification of angelic interlocutors or pre-existent Wisdom/Torah as addressees represents a sophisticated monotheistic apologetic that preserves textual plurality while containing its theological implications. This pattern illuminates how inner-biblical theological development became entangled with inter-religious polemic in the tradents' revisionist work.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: The Aramaic translations reveal that Jewish interpreters were not just worried about making God sound too human—they were also blocking Christian arguments that the plural 'us' proved the Trinity. By explaining that God was talking to angels or to Wisdom, they defended both God's incorporeality and Jewish monotheism against Christian interpretation.
